posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Vinfolio Blog – 28 Sep 24

Burgundy 2023: first look - Vinfolio Blog

Ahead of our full report on Burgundy 2023, here’s everything we know about the vintage, the growing season and the wines produced

Est. reading time: 9 minutes

link_counts.title: I think we need to talk about 2020 Red Burgundy

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not really an account of the season that I can relate to. The disease pressure during the growing season, while not as high as in 2024 (which broke every record), was challengingly high; and botrytis and acetic rot were issues in over-cropped Pinot at harvest. There are also plenty of wines above 13.5% (along with some that struggled to reach the AOC minimum).

posts.name: Tuomas Sarkola
posts.cooked: I guess it’s a bit early, but is the quality assessment in the ballpark? I think the article gives an impression that it will be a strong vintage for whites, little bit less for the reds and there is a high level of producer-variance.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I have tasted quite a few 2023s, but I’d still prefer to taste a bit more before giving an opinion. The wines aren’t being offered yet, so we have a bit of time…

posts.name: Ardeis Scott
posts.cooked: Not having the time to read at the moment—more cleanup duties from Helene. But curious, any comparisons to 2022?

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: From what I’ve tasted, it may be a good vintage for whites, but not great and won’t best the 22. Same with reds, likely not better than 22 in most cases.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

acetic rot

This is a phrase that I have not heard before. Is it akin to bird peck character? ie grape skins compromised and begin to rot?

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Kent_Comley:

This is a phrase that I have not heard before. Is it akin to bird peck character? ie grape skins compromised and begin to rot?

It sounds perhaps like over-cropped fruit + some moisture and now where for it to go.

This was a problem in some vineyards in Santa Barbara County in 2023 as well - you’d smell it as you walked through certain blocks.

Cheers

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Thanks. Okay makes sense, berry split caused by excessive rain close to harvest another cause.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yeah, when fruit compromised one way or another begins to botrytis with an appreciable presence of bacterial spoilage at the same time. Not pretty…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It will be more uneven than 2022 I think it’s fair to say. How high the highs will be is TBC. 2024 volumes are lower than 2021, so it will be nice to have some volume in 2023.

posts.name: Jordan Abraham
posts.cooked:
Most of 2023s I’ve tasted this year remind me of 2008…
Although many people that I trust and know to be thoughtful tasters seem to love 23 and don’t see a comparison to 2008

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: The reds remind me a lot of 2000. I love 2000.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yeah, I think that’s more apropos than 2008, a very high acidity (inc high malic acid) vintage.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Yeah, I think that’s more apropos than 2008, a very high acidity (inc high malic acid) vintage.

I’m in!

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Domaines were really high on the vintage at all of our tastings in May.
Of course they were also trying to cope with ‘24s.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: The 23s that I tasted last year were charming, but definitely softer than 22 or 20. I think the reds will be lovely and ready earlier and the whites will be average to average plus - right in line with 2000. Not a vintage I’m going super deep on, and leaning more towards the reds, but bought a nice selection of the usual suspects.

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked: Drinking a Berthaut-Gerbet Chambolle 1er Les Plantes 2023 at the moment and it is singing! I very much dislike 2022 (and 2020) for early drinking - too dense and dark-fruited for my liking - 2023 seems much more to my liking. Not as light-footed and delicate as the 17s and 21s, but with better acidity/freshness to complement the heady fruit that 22 and 20. No clue what will be best long-term, but for early drinking I much prefer 23.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked: In my recent experiences, 2022s across the board are far more enjoyable for current drinking, open and fresh; and not nearly as dark or monolithic as 2020s, which will need a decade or 3 to come around. Obviously there will be exceptions, but I’m much more inclined to lump 2020 and 2018 together. I think 22/23 are more similar to one another so far. YMMV

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked:
I can definitely see why people would find the 22s more enjoyable than the 20s - I just don’t like either. 22s might be more fresh and slightly brighter than the 20s, but to me the density of fruit in both makes them thoroughly unenjoyable. I have seen a lot of people share your sentiment though, so I am probably in the minority here.

Going through a good chunk of 22s (bought for testing) made me question my love for red Burgundy… luckily a few 21s re-established it, and the Plantes 23 made me believe in new vintages again =) Haven’t had a lot of 23s yet though, so it might be an outlier for me - testing a few more next weekend.
